elements.sent-2411-001: With GW170817 being the only multimessenger gravitational wave (GW) event with an associated kilonova (KN) detected so far, there exists a pressing need for realistic estimation of the GW localization uncertainties and rates, as well as optimization of available telescope time to enable the detection of new KNe.
elements.sent-2411-002: For this purpose, we simulate GW events assuming a data-driven, GW-motivated distribution of binary parameters for the LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA (LVK) fourth and fifth observing runs (O4 and O5).
elements.sent-2411-003: For our particular case of estimating KN detection rates in the O4 and O5 runs, we map the binary neutron star (BNS) and neutron star-black hole (NSBH) properties to the optical light curves arising from r-process nucleosynthesis in the ejecta.
elements.sent-2411-004: We use the simulated population of KNe to generate follow-up observing plans, with the primary goal of optimizing detection with the Gravitational Wave Multi-Messenger Astronomy DECam Survey (GW-MMADS).
elements.sent-2411-005: We explore KN detectability as a function of mass, distance, and spin of the binaries.
elements.sent-2411-006: Out of the mergers that produce a KN in our simulations, we expect detectable KNe for DECam-like instruments at a per-year rate of: $1-16$ ($0-1$) for BNS (NSBH) in O4, and $16-165$ ($1-12$) for BNS (NSBH) in O5, using a fiducial exposure time and conditional on the uncertainty on the equation of state (EOS) and volumetric rates of the mergers.
elements.sent-2411-007: Taking into account observability constraints, our scheduler covers the location of the KN $\sim 30-38\%$ of the times for our fiducial EOS.
elements.sent-2411-008: We provide the depths needed to detect a significant fraction of our simulated mergers for the astronomical community to use in their follow-up campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-009: In 2017, a binary neutron star (BNS) merger event was detected in gravitational waves (GWs), GW170817 \cite{ligobns , and it was accompanied by multiple electromagnetic (EM) counterparts \cite{MMApaper .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The detection of its EM counterparts, namely the gamma-ray burst GRB 170817A \cite{LIGOScientific:2017zic , the kilonova (KN) AT2017gfo \cite{Coulter1556,Soares_Santos_2017 , and later the afterglow \cite{Fong2019,Makhathini_2021 , opened a new era of multimessenger astronomy \cite{margutti_review .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Kilonovae \cite{metzger_kilonovae are transients ranging from the infrared to the ultraviolet powered by the radioactive decay of heavy nuclei synthesized in the highly-neutronized material ejected during neutron star (NS) mergers.}
elements.sent-2411-012: These transients are considered to provide smoking-gun evidence for BNS or neutron star-black hole (NSBH) mergers.
elements.sent-2411-013: The detection of the KN AT2017gfo enabled hundreds of multimessenger analyses on the theory of gravity \cite{Belgacem_2018 , the Universe’s expansion \cite{2017Natur.551...85A,Palmese:2023beh,Hotokezaka19,2022Univ....8..289B , the formation and fate of compact objects \cite{Blanchard_2017,palmese,tsai2020gw170817,Kilpatrick_2022 , thanks to its precise localization in the sky, which allowed us to pinpoint the host galaxy of the GW merger.}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, KN observations themselves provide us with information on the physics of NS mergers, including but not limited to their composition \cite{anand2023chemical,2019Natur.574..497W,2021ApJ...913...26D,2023MNRAS.526L.155H,2023ApJ...944..123V,2023A A...675A.194S , EOS \cite{Coughlin_2018,P_rez_Garc_a_2022,Margalit_2019,Dietrich_2020 , fate of the remnant object \cite{Yu_2018 and origin of heavy elements (generated by r-process nucleosynthesis in the neutron-rich ejecta of the KNe \cite{Kasen_2017 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The wide variety of possible multimessenger analyses from the identification of a KN motivates us to optimize the follow-up of BNS and NSBH merger events to characterize multimessenger sources beyond GW170817.
elements.sent-2411-016: Currently, the international GW network consisting of the advanced LIGO~\cite{2018LRR....21....3A , advanced Virgo~\cite{VIRGO:2014yos , and KAGRA~\cite{KAGRA:2020tym interferometers (LVK) is carrying out its fourth observing run (O4).}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Although the LVK detected a few BNS \cite{Abbott_2020_GW190425 and NSBH \cite{Abbott_2021,Abbott_2023 candidates at different confidence levels in O3, there has not been a single multimessenger KN detection since the second observing run (O2) \cite{Anand_2020,2019ApJ...884L..55G,Andreoni_2020 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-439: We use the simulated population of kilonovae to generate follow-up observing plans, with the primary goal of optimizing detection with the Gravitational Wave Multi-Messenger Astronomy DECam Survey (GW-MMADS).
elements.sent-2411-440: We explore the dependence of kilonova detectability on the mass, distance, inclination, and spin of the binaries.
elements.sent-2411-441: Assuming that no BNS was detected during O4 until the end of 2024, we present updated GW BNS (NSBH) merger detection rates.
elements.sent-2411-442: We expect to detect BNS (NSBH) kilonovae with DECam at a per-year rate of: $0$--$2.0$ ($0$) in O4, and $2.0$--$19$ ($0$--$1.0$) in O5.
elements.sent-2411-443: We expect the majority of BNS detections and also those accompanied by a detectable kilonova to produce a hypermassive NS remnant, with a significant fraction of the remaining BNSs promptly collapsing to a BH.
elements.sent-2411-444: We release GW simulations and depths required to detect kilonovae based on our predictions to support the astronomical community in their multimessenger follow-up campaigns and analyses.
elements.sent-2411-445: In 2017, a binary neutron star (BNS) merger event was detected in gravitational waves (GWs), GW170817 \citep{ligobns , and it was accompanied by multiple electromagnetic (EM) counterparts \citep{MMApaper .}}

elements.sent-2411-447: Kilonovae \citep{Rosswog_2005,2013ApJ...775...18B,2014MNRAS.439..757G,metzger_kilonovae are transients ranging from the infrared to the ultraviolet powered by the radioactive decay of heavy nuclei synthesized in the highly-neutronized material ejected during neutron star (NS) mergers.}
